codeReview.summary: The feature is substantially complete and correct. The two-step exit confirmation, session recorder, focus-mode coexistence, and all required tests are present and working. All 131 unit tests and 6 UI tests pass. Two non-blocking issues were found: (1) DeepFocusSessionView is dead code that duplicates exit dialog logic but is never instantiated, and (2) a narrow race condition exists where orphaned-session auto-completion on relaunch may not trigger stat recording because onSessionCompleted is wired in DeepFocusTabView's .task (which only runs when the Deep Focus tab is selected), but recoverOrphanedSession() is called in MainTabView's .task at launch.

issues.description: DeepFocusSessionView is dead code — compiled (registered in project.pbxproj) but never instantiated anywhere in the production codebase. This commit added onSessionExitConfirmed callback and two-step dialog logic to it, but DeepFocusTabView only ever shows DeepFocusLauncherView (not DeepFocusSessionView) for active sessions. The exit logic in DeepFocusSessionView is unreachable. Not a correctness bug for the shipped feature, but misleading for future workers.

Race condition in orphaned-session auto-completion stat recording. In MainTabView.task (ContentView.swift ~line 98), sessionManager.recoverOrphanedSession() is called on every launch. If the session elapsed fully while the app was dead, recoverOrphanedSession() calls completeSession() → onSessionCompleted?(). However, onSessionCompleted is only set inside DeepFocusTabView.task (DeepFocusTabView.swift ~line 77), which only fires when the Deep Focus tab is selected. Since the initial tab is .focus (not .deepFocus), onSessionCompleted is nil at the moment of auto-completion, so handleSessionCompletion() is never called and stats are not recorded. Unit tests bypass this by setting the callback before calling recoverOrphanedSession(). Narrow edge case (app killed + session fully elapsed + user relaunches without visiting Deep Focus tab), but means the VAL-CROSS-010 stat-recording guarantee is not met in production for that scenario.

recordAbandonedSession(sessionID:startTime:configuredDuration:remainingSeconds:...) accepts an arbitrary remainingSeconds value, allowing callers to record non-zero completed focus time for abandoned sessions. The spec requires 'completedFocusTime=0' for abandoned sessions. The production code path (recordSession(from:)) correctly hardcodes remainingSeconds=configuredDuration for abandoned sessions, but the standalone method is inconsistent with the spec. It could mislead future callers into accidentally recording partial focus credit for abandoned sessions.

Test named 'terminatedSessionRecordedAsAbandoned' (AppTerminationDuringDeepFocusTests suite) asserts sessionStatus == .active, not .abandoned. The test verifies resumption of a still-running session, not abandonment. The test name is misleading; a matching test for the case where a session is actually recorded as abandoned after relaunch (time fully elapsed but checking SwiftData record) is absent from this suite.

Unit tests cancelFirstConfirmation and cancelSecondConfirmation (SessionExitTests) are trivially passing — they just verify the session manager is still .active after starting a session without triggering any cancellation code. These tests do not exercise the alert dismiss path (that's correctly covered by the UI tests), but their names suggest they do. The tests would pass regardless of whether the confirmation dialog exists.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The onSessionCompleted callback is wired inside a SwiftUI .task modifier on a non-default tab view (DeepFocusTabView), but the recovery call that fires the callback is in the parent MainTabView's .task. This creates an implicit ordering dependency: any code that needs the callback set must run after the Deep Focus tab is first visited. This pattern is fragile and not documented in AGENTS.md. Future workers building on top of onSessionCompleted (e.g., for completion analytics) should be warned that the callback is not set until the tab is selected.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: ContentView.swift ~line 98: sessionManager.recoverOrphanedSession() in MainTabView.task; DeepFocusTabView.swift ~line 77: sessionManager.onSessionCompleted = { handleSessionCompletion() } in DeepFocusTabView.task. Initial selectedTab = .focus means DeepFocusTabView's task fires only when the Deep Focus tab is visited.

sharedStateObservations.observation: DeepFocusSessionView.swift is a compiled but dead-code file. It duplicates the two-step exit dialog logic and onSessionExitConfirmed callback from DeepFocusLauncherView but is never used. DeepFocusTabView always routes to DeepFocusLauncherView (not DeepFocusSessionView) when isSessionRunning is true. AGENTS.md does not warn about this. Future workers may add code or tests targeting DeepFocusSessionView incorrectly.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Grep for 'DeepFocusSessionView' in .swift files returns only the file's own definition. DeepFocusTabView.swift body renders DeepFocusLauncherView when sessionManager.isSessionRunning, never DeepFocusSessionView.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The skill procedure (step 3: write tests first / TDD) was not strictly followed — the worker's transcript shows that most of the production code was already present before tests were written (the baseline unit test run showed 131 tests passing before the worker made changes, meaning the code was pre-existing from a prior worker session). The worker verified rather than wrote-then-implemented. skillFeedback.followedProcedure was reported as true, but the transcript shows no failing-test-first step. This deviation was harmless (feature was partially pre-built), but the skill procedure description for step 3 may need to account for 'feature already partially implemented' scenarios.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Transcript: 'Let me run the baseline tests to check the current state' → exit code 0, 131 tests in 9 suites passed. Worker then confirmed all code was already in place. No failing-then-passing test cycle shown.

summary: The deep-focus-session-exit-completion feature passes review. All required behaviors are implemented: two-step exit confirmation with both cancel paths, session completion cascade, DeepFocusSessionRecorder with correct completedFocusTime=0 for abandoned sessions, focus-mode coexistence via independent named stores, and comprehensive unit + UI test coverage for all five validation assertions. Five non-blocking issues were found: dead code in DeepFocusSessionView, a narrow race condition in orphaned-session auto-completion stat recording (onSessionCompleted not yet set when relaunch recovery fires on non-Deep-Focus tab), the standalone recordAbandonedSession method not enforcing completedFocusTime=0, a misleadingly named unit test, and trivially-passing cancel unit tests. None of these block the core functionality.
